metadata.title: The Song That Lied to You: How Music Encodes Deception

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. A catchy melody can encode a claim and make it feel true before you ever check it. The nursery-rhyme link to the Great Plague of 1665 is a myth people repeat because it thrills. You felt the thrill, told someone, and the false history spread through the song's rhythm. Music is powerful pedagogy, which is exactly why it can cement deception so efficiently.

props.artifactTitle: The song that lied to you
artifactLines: A catchy melody can encode a claim and make it feel true before you ever check it.
The nursery-rhyme link to the Great Plague of 1665 is a myth people repeat because it thrills.
You felt the thrill, told someone, and the false history spread through the song's rhythm.
Music is powerful pedagogy, which is exactly why it can cement deception so efficiently.

beats.narration_text: I want to test whether a melody can smuggle a false claim past my skepticism. Take the plague myth attached to that nursery rhyme and help me trace how it actually spread. Show me: (1) how a catchy rhythmic structure makes a dubious historical claim feel already-verified, (2) two other songs whose lyrics encode a widely-believed but debunked story, and (3) a quick checklist I can run when a tune tempts me to repeat its 'fact' without checking.

output: You learned it before you could read
It was about the Black Death
